---
author: "Equipo EDUMER"
bibliography: "../input/bib/merit-factorial.bib"
csl: "../input/bib/apa6.csl"
---

# Introduction


Despite rising economic inequality and limited social mobility in contemporary societies [@chancel_world_2025; @lopez-roldan_comparative_2021], meritocratic beliefs remain remarkably widespread among citizens [@mijs_paradox_2021]. Meritocracy refers to the idea that social rewards are, and should be, allocated according to individual effort and ability rather than social origins or personal connections [@young_rise_1958]. This persistence is sociologically consequential because meritocratic beliefs shape how people interpret inequality and fairness in unequal societies [@madeira_primes_2019; @sandel_tyranny_2020]. Schools are especially important in this regard, as they are a key site where meritocratic ideals are formed, reinforced, and experienced early in life [@resh_sense_2014; @mijs_unfulfillable_2016; @batruch_belief_2022]. Understanding how young people perceive and endorse meritocratic beliefs is therefore crucial for tracing the emergence of judgments about fairness and inequality during early socialization. 

As research on the causes and consequences of meritocratic beliefs has expanded, so too has debate over how they should be conceptualized and measured. This debate reflects growing recognition that meritocracy is not a unitary construct, but one composed of multiple, analytically distinct elements. Survey-based studies nevertheless use highly heterogeneous measures, ranging from broad beliefs about system justification and social mobility to attitudes toward individual responsibility and structural constraints [@mccoy_priming_2007; @castillo_meritocracia_2019]. Much of this literature relies on single-dimensional indicators, often conflates descriptive perceptions (how meritocracy works) with normative preferences (how it should work) [e.g., @liu_does_2025; @tang_meritocratic_2025], and frequently reduces meritocracy to the social valuation of effort [e.g., @mijs_paradox_2021; @wiederkehr_belief_2015], overlooking other meritocratic components. @chauvin_school_2026 recently advanced this debate by proposing a multidimensional scale of school meritocracy that distinguishes a general factor from specific ability and equity dimensions. Yet their model leaves important conceptual issues unresolved: it combines school-specific dimensions with a broader meritocracy factor on the same scale, and it remains limited to perceived meritocracy. A related problem concerns the treatment of non-meritocratic principles [see also @mijs_visualizing_2026, for a recent review]. Even when researchers distinguish meritocratic from non-meritocratic factors (e.g., family wealth or social connections), these are often collapsed into a single continuum through subtraction or reverse coding [e.g., @reynolds_perceptions_2014a; @wiesner_effort_2026], imposing a theoretically strong and empirically problematic zero-sum assumption. If meritocratic and non-meritocratic beliefs can coexist rather than function as strict opposites, greater recognition of non-meritocratic barriers may be misread as weaker endorsement of meritocracy [@zhu_meritocratic_2025]. To address these limitations, this paper adopts the multidimensional framework proposed by Castillo et al. [-@castillo_multidimensional_2023], which distinguishes between perceptions and preferences, and between meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles. Although supported in adult populations, this framework has not yet been tested among school-aged students in everyday school contexts.

The meaning of meritocracy is especially consequential in school settings, where it operates not only as a cultural ideal but also as a central organizing principle of institutional life. Schools promote meritocracy as a normative ideal by presenting education as a legitimate pathway to upward mobility and by framing individual effort and achievement as the proper basis of social reward [@vandewerfhorst_meritocracy_2024]. Yet this promise coexists with persistent inequalities in later-life outcomes and with evidence that schooling often reproduces inequality through stratified and unequal learning environments [@bourdieu_reproduction_1990; @goudeau_hidden_2017a; @zhou_equalization_2019]. At the same time, students encounter meritocracy not only as discourse but also as a lived experience through grading, selection, and tracking, which make “merit” visible and consequential [@traini_stratification_2022; @darnon_where_2018; @resh_sense_2014; @tang_meritocratic_2025]. Research further shows that stronger endorsement of meritocratic beliefs in school is associated with greater justification of inequality [@batruch_belief_2022; @wiederkehr_belief_2015; @lampert_meritocratic_2013], even as students may simultaneously recognize structural constraints, reflecting a form of “dual consciousness” [@liu_does_2025]. Taken together, these tensions make schools a crucial setting for examining how adolescents understand meritocracy, non-meritocratic constraints, and the fairness of unequal outcomes.

Against this background, this paper examines whether school-aged students conceptualize meritocratic beliefs in multidimensional terms. Building on the framework proposed by Castillo et al. [-@castillo_multidimensional_2023], we extend this approach to adolescents in everyday school contexts. Specifically, we ask:

(1) Do school-aged students distinguish between meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles, as well as between perceptions (“what is”) and preferences (“what should be”)?

(2) Can these dimensions be measured in ways that are comparable across age cohorts and stable over time?

We argue that a multidimensional conception of meritocratic beliefs clarifies what adolescents actually endorse—descriptions, ideals, or both—and provides a stronger basis for valid developmental and longitudinal analysis. To examine this, we use novel survey data and test measurement invariance across two age cohorts (8th and 10th graders) and two waves (2023 and 2024), thereby assessing the stability and comparability of these dimensions across groups and over time.
